Create a complete beginner-friendly study guide on Git and GitHub.

The guide should be written in very simple, human, easy-to-understand language — not in official documentation style, not too technical, and not full of difficult jargon.

The tone should feel like:
- a kind teacher
- explaining patiently
- using very simple real-life examples
- like teaching a beginner who has never used Git or GitHub before
- like explaining to someone from a small town or village in a very practical and respectful way using day-to-day analogies

The guide should help:
- new developers
- Power Platform developers
- app makers
- solution architects
- technical beginners
- anyone starting with source control

## Main Goal
The guide should explain:
1. What Git is
2. What GitHub is
3. Why Git is used in real projects
4. Why GitHub is used in team projects
5. Difference between Git and GitHub
6. How Git works in simple language
7. Why developers should learn Git
8. How architects and senior developers use Git in projects
9. How a beginner should start using Git step by step
10. What common Git commands mean and when to use them

## Writing Style Requirements
- Use very simple English
- Avoid heavy technical words unless necessary
- If a technical word is used, explain it immediately in plain language
- Use many real-life analogies
- Use examples like notebook, school project, shop register, folder, draft copy, team work, teacher checking homework, etc.
- Do not write like official documentation
- Do not assume prior knowledge
- Explain every important command in plain language
- Make the learner feel confident, not scared

## Structure of the Study Guide

Create the guide with these sections:

### 1. Friendly Introduction
- Welcome the learner
- Explain why Git looks confusing in the beginning
- Reassure them that it becomes easy
- Explain the purpose of the guide

### 2. What is Git?
Explain in very simple language:
- Git is a version control tool
- What “version control” means in simple life terms
- Why Git is useful
- How Git helps save history of code
- How Git helps when something breaks
- How Git helps during teamwork

### 3. What is GitHub?
Explain:
- GitHub is a website/platform where Git-based projects are stored online
- Why GitHub is useful for teams
- How GitHub helps in backup, collaboration, review, and sharing
- Difference between local machine and GitHub online repository

### 4. Git vs GitHub
Explain clearly:
- Git = tool
- GitHub = platform
- why both are related but not the same
- use a very simple analogy

### 5. Why We Use Git in Real Projects
Explain practical reasons such as:
- tracking code changes
- team collaboration
- rollback
- safe experimentation
- branching
- release management
- debugging with history
- code reviews
- working on features without breaking main code

### 6. Basic Important Terms
Explain each term in very simple language:
- repository
- commit
- branch
- merge
- clone
- push
- pull
- fetch
- origin
- checkout / switch
- pull request
- merge conflict
- staging area
- working directory
- remote
- main / master
- README
- .gitignore

Each term should have:
- simple definition
- real-life analogy
- where this term is used in actual work

### 7. How Git Works – Step by Step
Explain the full flow in simple sequence:
- create a folder/project
- initialize git
- add files
- commit changes
- connect to GitHub
- push code
- create branch
- make changes
- commit again
- push branch
- create pull request
- merge changes

### 8. First-Time Setup
Explain:
- how to install Git
- how to check Git version
- how to configure username and email
- why this setup is needed

Include ready-to-use commands.

### 9. Git Commands Cheat Sheet for Beginners
Give a dedicated section with most-used commands.

For every command, provide:
- command
- what it does
- when to use it
- sample example
- easy explanation

Must include at least these commands:
- git --version
- git config --global user.name
- git config --global user.email
- git init
- git clone
- git status
- git add .
- git add <file>
- git commit -m "message"
- git log
- git diff
- git branch
- git switch
- git checkout
- git merge
- git pull
- git push
- git fetch
- git remote -v
- git remote add origin
- git stash
- git stash pop
- git reset
- git revert

### 10. Ready-to-Use Real Project Scenarios
Create practical beginner examples such as:

#### Scenario A: Starting a new project
Show exact commands from beginning

#### Scenario B: Downloading an existing project from GitHub
Show commands and explain what happens

#### Scenario C: Making a small change and sending it to GitHub
Show step-by-step flow

#### Scenario D: Working on a new feature using a branch
Show exact commands

#### Scenario E: Pulling latest changes from team repository
Show exact commands

#### Scenario F: Fixing a mistake using Git history
Show beginner-safe explanation

### 11. Daily Workflow for a Developer
Give a simple “daily use” routine like:
- pull latest code
- create/switch branch
- work on changes
- check status
- add changes
- commit
- push
- create pull request

Explain like a daily habit.

### 12. Daily Workflow for an Architect / Senior Developer
Explain how an architect or senior developer may use Git differently:
- reviewing branches
- checking pull requests
- understanding history
- release branch awareness
- reading diffs
- traceability
- collaboration with team

Keep it simple and practical.

### 13. Common Mistakes Beginners Make
Examples:
- committing directly to main
- forgetting to pull latest changes
- bad commit messages
- not using branches
- pushing unfinished work
- confusion between Git and GitHub
- forgetting .gitignore
- panic during merge conflict

For each mistake:
- explain what it is
- why it happens
- how to avoid it

### 14. Merge Conflict in Simple Language
Explain:
- what it is
- why it happens
- when it happens
- how to solve it in beginner-friendly steps
- give a very simple example

### 15. Best Practices
Give easy practical best practices:
- commit often
- write meaningful commit messages
- use branches
- pull before push
- don’t commit secrets/passwords
- keep repository clean
- use README
- use .gitignore
- don’t fear Git history

### 16. Beginner FAQ
Include common questions like:
- Is Git difficult?
- Do I need GitHub to use Git?
- Can I undo mistakes?
- What if I commit wrong code?
- What if I deleted a file?
- Why branches are useful?
- What is the safest beginner workflow?

### 17. 7-Day Learning Plan
Make a beginner-friendly short learning plan:
- Day 1: Understand Git and GitHub
- Day 2: Setup Git
- Day 3: Init, add, commit
- Day 4: GitHub push/pull
- Day 5: Branching
- Day 6: Merge/conflicts
- Day 7: Real project workflow practice

### 18. Final Revision Section
Create:
- quick recap
- mini command summary
- “if you remember only 10 commands, remember these” list
- confidence message for beginners

## Output Format Requirements
The guide should be:
- well structured
- easy to scan
- highly readable
- beginner-friendly
- practical
- full of examples

Use:
- headings
- bullet points
- short paragraphs
- code blocks
- step-by-step instructions
- mini examples
- notes
- pro tips
- warning boxes

## Command Formatting Requirement
Whenever you show commands:
- put them in proper code blocks
- explain below the code block what each command does
- show realistic examples with folder/project names

## Teaching Requirement
Act like a patient trainer.
Do not rush.
Do not assume knowledge.
Explain everything from zero.
Use simple analogies repeatedly where helpful.

## Important Restriction
Do NOT make the study guide sound like copied official documentation.
Do NOT make it too formal.
Do NOT make it too advanced.
Do NOT overload with deep theory.
Do NOT skip beginner doubts.

## Final Quality Goal
The final guide should be so simple that a beginner can say:
“Now I understand what Git is, what GitHub is, and how to use the common commands in my real project.”

At the end, include:
- Git command cheat sheet
- one full mini project example
- one sample branching workflow
- one sample pull request workflow
- one beginner practice exercise

Write the entire guide in plain, friendly, practical language.
